title: What Is Not A Common Transmission Route For Bloodborne Pathogens
source_filename: What Is Not A Common Transmission Route For Bloodborne Pathogens.docx

qas.q: Bloodborne pathogens can be which of the following types of microorganisms?
Which of the following is NOT a common transmission route for bloodborne pathogens?
Which of the following is least likely to contain bloodborne pathogens?
Why are the eyes, nose, and mouth common transmission routes for bloodborne pathogens?
qas.a: They are all examples of mucous membranes

qas.q: Which of the following most accurately describes the 2 main sources of bloodborne pathogens?
What is the minimum level of personal protective equipment (PPE) you need to wear to protect against a potential bloodborne pathogen exposure?
What is the proper technique to use when removing an item of personal protective equipment (PPE)?
qas.a: Turn the item inside out as you remove it (to contain any contaminants)

qas.q: Where should you dispose of needles and other sharp instruments when you are done using them?
What is an important guideline to remember when you are responding to an emergency that may involve bloodborne pathogens?
qas.a: Protect yourself first, and then treat the victim

qas.q: What is the approach to infection control in which all human blood and certain human body fluids are treated as if they are known to be infectious?
To whom should you immediately report a potential bloodborne pathogen exposure?
What is the first thing you should do if you think an area of your body may have been exposed to bloodborne pathogens?
qas.a: Flood the exposed area with water
